--- SOURCE 1: History of nanotechnology - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_nanotechnology

SUMMARY:
Nanotechnology and nanoscience got a boost in the early 1980s with two major developments: the birth of cluster science and the invention of the scanning tunneling microscope (STM) . These developments led to the discovery of fullerenes in 1985 and the structural assignment of carbon nanotubes in 1991.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://en.wikipedia.org/static/images/donate/donate.gif)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

# History of nanotechnology

The **history of nanotechnology** traces the development of the concepts and experimental work falling under the broad category of [nanotechnology](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology"). Although nanotechnology is a relatively recent development in scientific research, the development of its central concepts happened over a longer period of time. The emergence of nanotechnology in the 1980s was caused by the convergence of experimental advances such as the invention of the [scanning tunneling microscope](/wiki/Scanning_tunneling_microscope "Scanning tunneling microscope") in 1981 and the discovery of [fullerenes](/wiki/Fullerene "Fullerene") in 1985, with the elucidation and popularization of a conceptual framework for the goals of nanotechnology beginning with the 1986 publication of the book *[Engines of Creation](/wiki/Engines_of_Creation "Engines of Creation")*. The field was subject to growing public awareness and controversy in the early 2000s, with prominent debates about both its [potential implications](/wiki/Implications_of_nanotechnology "Implications of nanotechnology") as well as the feasibility of the applications envisioned by advocates of [molecular nanotechnology](/wiki/Molecular_nanotechnology "Molecular nanotechnology"), and with governments moving to promote and [fund research](/wiki/Funding_of_science "Funding of science") into nanotechnology. The early 2000s also saw the beginnings of commercial [applications of nanotechnology](/wiki/List_of_nanotechnology_applications "List of nanotechnology applications"), although these were limited to bulk applications of [nanomaterials](/wiki/Nanomaterials "Nanomaterials") rather than the [transformative](/wiki/Disruptive_technology "Disruptive technology") applications envisioned by the field.

## Contents

## Early uses of nanomaterials

[Carbon nanotubes](/wiki/Carbon_nanotube "Carbon nanotube") have been found in pottery from [Keeladi](/wiki/Keeladi "Keeladi"), India, dating to c. 600–300 BC, though it is not known how they formed or whether the substance containing them was employed deliberately.[[1]](#cite_note-1) [Cementite](/wiki/Cementite "Cementite") [nanowires](/wiki/Nanowires "Nanowires") have been observed in [Damascus steel](/wiki/Damascus_steel "Damascus steel"), a material dating back to c. 900 AD, their origin and means of manufacture also unknown.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Although nanoparticles are associated with modern science, they were used by [artisans](/wiki/Artisan "Artisan") as far back as the ninth century in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") for creating a [glittering](/wiki/Lusterware "Lusterware") effect on the surface of pots.[[3]](#cite_note-reiss-3)[[4]](#cite_note-khan-4)

In modern times, [pottery](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery") from the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") often retains a distinct gold- or copper-colored metallic glitter. This [luster](/wiki/Lusterware "Lusterware") is caused by a metallic film that was applied to the transparent surface of a [glazing](/wiki/Glazing_(window) "Glazing (window)"), which contains [silver](/wiki/Silver_nanoparticle "Silver nanoparticle") and [copper nanoparticles](/wiki/Copper_nanoparticle "Copper nanoparticle") dispersed homogeneously in the glassy matrix of the ceramic glaze. These nanoparticles are created by the artisans by adding [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") and [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver") [salts](/wiki/Salt_(chemistry) "Salt (chemistry)") and [oxides](/wiki/Oxide "Oxide") together with [vinegar](/wiki/Vinegar "Vinegar"), [ochre](/wiki/Ochre "Ochre"), and [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay") on the surface of previously glazed pottery. The technique originated in the [Muslim world](/wiki/Muslim_world "Muslim world"). As Muslims were not allowed to use gold in artistic representations, they sought a way to create a similar effect without using real gold. The solution they found was using luster.[[4]](#cite_note-khan-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Conceptual origins

### Richard Feynman

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/42/Richard_Feynman_Nobel.jpg/250px-Richard_Feynman_Nobel.jpg)

The American [physicist](/wiki/Physicist "Physicist") [Richard Feynman](/wiki/Richard_Feynman "Richard Feynman") lectured, "[There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom](/wiki/There%27s_Plenty_of_Room_at_the_Bottom "There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom")," at an [American Physical Society](/wiki/American_Physical_Society "American Physical Society") meeting at [Caltech](/wiki/Caltech "Caltech") on December 29, 1959, which is often held to have provided inspiration for the field of [nanotechnology](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology"). Feynman had described a process by which the ability to manipulate individual atoms and molecules might be developed, using one set of precise tools to build and operate another proportionally smaller set, so on down to the needed scale. In the course of this, he noted, scaling issues would arise from the changing magnitude of various physical phenomena: gravity would become less important, [surface tension](/wiki/Surface_tension "Surface tension") and [Van der Waals attraction](/wiki/Van_der_Waals_force "Van der Waals force") would become more important.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

After Feynman's death, a scholar studying the historical development of nanotechnology has concluded that his actual role in catalyzing nanotechnology research was limited, based on recollections from many of the people active in the nascent field in the 1980s and 1990s. Chris Toumey, a [cultural anthropologist](/wiki/Cultural_anthropologist "Cultural anthropologist") at the [University of South Carolina](/wiki/University_of_South_Carolina "University of South Carolina"), found that the published versions of Feynman's talk had a negligible influence in the twenty years after it was first published, as measured by citations in the scientific literature, and not much more influence in the decade after the [Scanning Tunneling Microscope](/wiki/Scanning_Tunneling_Microscope "Scanning Tunneling Microscope") was invented in 1981. Subsequently, interest in “Plenty of Room” in the scientific literature greatly increased in the early 1990s. This is probably because the term “nanotechnology” gained serious attention just before that time, following its use by [K. Eric Drexler](/wiki/K._Eric_Drexler "K. Eric Drexler") in his 1986 book, [*Engines of Creation: The Coming Era of Nanotechnology*](/wiki/Engines_of_Creation "Engines of Creation"), which took the Feynman concept of a billion tiny factories and added the idea that they could make more copies of themselves via computer control instead of control by a human operator; and in a cover article headlined "Nanotechnology",[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) published later that year in a mass-circulation science-oriented magazine, *[Omni](/wiki/Omni_(magazine) "Omni (magazine)")*. Toumey's analysis also includes comments from distinguished scientists in nanotechnology who say that “Plenty of Room” did not influence their early work, and in fact most of them had not read it until a later date.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

These and other developments hint that the retroactive rediscovery of Feynman's “Plenty of Room” gave nanotechnology a packaged history that provided an early date of December 1959, plus a connection to the charisma and genius of Richard Feynman. Feynman's stature as a Nobel laureate and as an iconic figure in 20th century science surely helped advocates of nanotechnology and provided a valuable intellectual link to the past.[[11]](#cite_note-Milburn-11)

### Norio Taniguchi

Japanese scientist Norio Taniguchi of [Tokyo University of Science](/wiki/Tokyo_University_of_Science "Tokyo University of Science") was the first to use the term "nano-technology" in a 1974 conference,[[12]](#cite_note-Taniguchi-12) to describe semiconductor processes such as thin film deposition and ion beam milling exhibiting characteristic control on the order of a nanometer. His definition was, "'Nano-technology' mainly consists of the processing of, separation, consolidation, and deformation of materials by one atom or one molecule." However, the term was not used again until 1981 when Eric Drexler, who was unaware of Taniguchi's prior use of the term, published his first paper on nanotechnology in 1981.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### K. Eric Drexler

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Eric_Drexler_2007.jpg/250px-Eric_Drexler_2007.jpg)

In the 1980s the idea of nanotechnology as a [deterministic](/wiki/Deterministic "Deterministic"), rather than [stochastic](/wiki/Stochastic "Stochastic"), handling of individual atoms and molecules was conceptually explored in depth by K. Eric Drexler, who promoted the technological significance of nano-scale phenomena and devices through speeches and two influential books.

In 1980, Drexler encountered Feynman's provocative 1959 talk "There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom" while preparing his initial scientific paper on the subject, “Molecular Engineering: An approach to the development of general capabilities for molecular manipulation,” published in the *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences* in 1981.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The term "nanotechnology" (which paralleled [Taniguchi's "nano-technology"](#Norio_Taniguchi)) was independently applied by Drexler in his 1986 book *[Engines of Creation: The Coming Era of Nanotechnology](/wiki/Engines_of_creation "Engines of creation")*, which proposed the idea of a nanoscale "assembler" which would be able to build a copy of itself and of other items of arbitrary complexity. He also first published the term "[grey goo](/wiki/Grey_goo "Grey goo")" to describe what might happen if a hypothetical [self-replicating machine](/wiki/Self-replicating_machine "Self-replicating machine"), capable of independent operation, were constructed and released. Drexler's vision of nanotechnology is often called "[Molecular Nanotechnology](/wiki/Molecular_Nanotechnology "Molecular Nanotechnology")" (MNT) or "molecular manufacturing."

His 1991 Ph.D. work at the [MIT Media Lab](/wiki/MIT_Media_Lab "MIT Media Lab") was the first doctoral degree on the topic of molecular nanotechnology and (after some editing) his thesis, "Molecular Machinery and Manufacturing with Applications to Computation,"[[17]](#cite_note-17) was published as *Nanosystems: Molecular Machinery, Manufacturing, and Computation,*[[18]](#cite_note-18) which received the Association of American Publishers award for Best Computer Science Book of 1992. Drexler founded the [Foresight Institute](/wiki/Foresight_Institute "Foresight Institute") in 1986 with the mission of "Preparing for nanotechnology.” Drexler is no longer a member of the Foresight Institute.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Experimental research and advances

In [nanoelectronics](/wiki/Nanoelectronics "Nanoelectronics"), nanoscale thickness was demonstrated in the [gate oxide](/wiki/Gate_oxide "Gate oxide") and [thin films](/wiki/Thin_films "Thin films") used in [transistors](/wiki/Transistors "Transistors") as early as the 1960s, but it was not until the late 1990s that [MOSFETs](/wiki/MOSFET "MOSFET") (metal–oxide–semiconductor field-effect transistors) with nanoscale [gate](/wiki/Metal_gate "Metal gate") length were demonstrated. Nanotechnology and [nanoscience](/wiki/Nanoscience "Nanoscience") got a boost in the early 1980s with two major developments: the birth of [cluster](/wiki/Cluster_(physics) "Cluster (physics)") science and the invention of the [scanning tunneling microscope](/wiki/Scanning_tunneling_microscope "Scanning tunneling microscope") (STM). These developments led to the discovery of [fullerenes](/wiki/Fullerenes "Fullerenes") in 1985 and the structural assignment of [carbon nanotubes](/wiki/Carbon_nanotubes "Carbon nanotubes") in 1991. The development of [FinFET](/wiki/FinFET "FinFET") in the 1990s aldo laid the foundations for modern [nanoelectronic](/wiki/Nanoelectronic "Nanoelectronic") [semiconductor device fabrication](/wiki/Semiconductor_device_fabrication "Semiconductor device fabrication").

### Invention of scanning probe microscopy

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Gerd_Binnig_sw.jpg/250px-Gerd_Binnig_sw.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/32/Rohrer.jpg)

The [scanning tunneling microscope](/wiki/Scanning_tunneling_microscope "Scanning tunneling microscope"), an instrument for imaging surfaces at the atomic level, was developed in 1981 by [Gerd Binnig](/wiki/Gerd_Binnig "Gerd Binnig") and [Heinrich Rohrer](/wiki/Heinrich_Rohrer "Heinrich Rohrer") at [IBM Zurich Research Laboratory](/wiki/IBM_Zurich_Research_Laboratory "IBM Zurich Research Laboratory"), for which they were awarded the [Nobel Prize in Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics") in 1986.[[19]](#cite_note-Binnig-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Binnig, [Calvin Quate](/wiki/Calvin_Quate "Calvin Quate") and [Christoph Gerber](/wiki/Christoph_Gerber "Christoph Gerber") invented the first [atomic force microscope](/wiki/Atomic_force_microscope "Atomic force microscope") in 1986. The first commercially available atomic force microscope was introduced in 1989.

[IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM") researcher [Don Eigler](/wiki/Don_Eigler "Don Eigler") was the first to manipulate atoms using a scanning tunneling microscope in 1989. He used 35 [Xenon](/wiki/Xenon "Xenon") atoms to [spell out the IBM logo](/wiki/IBM_(atoms) "IBM (atoms)").[[21]](#cite_note-21) He shared the 2010 [Kavli Prize](/wiki/Kavli_Prize "Kavli Prize") in Nanoscience for this work.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Advances in interface and colloid science

[Interface and colloid science](/wiki/Interface_and_colloid_science "Interface and colloid science") had existed for nearly a century before they became associated with nanotechnology.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) The first observations and size measurements of nanoparticles had been made during the first decade of the 20th century by [Richard Adolf Zsigmondy](/wiki/Richard_Adolf_Zsigmondy "Richard Adolf Zsigmondy"), winner of the 1925 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry"), who made a detailed study of gold sols and other [nanomaterials](/wiki/Nanomaterials "Nanomaterials") with sizes down to 10 nm using an [ultramicroscope](/wiki/Ultramicroscope "Ultramicroscope") which was capable of visualizing particles much smaller than the [light](/wiki/Light "Light") [wavelength](/wiki/Wavelength "Wavelength").[[25]](#cite_note-25) Zsigmondy was also the first to use the term "nanometer" explicitly for characterizing particle size. In the 1920s, [Irving Langmuir](/wiki/Irving_Langmuir "Irving Langmuir"), winner of the 1932 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, and [Katharine B. Blodgett](/wiki/Katharine_B._Blodgett "Katharine B. Blodgett") introduced the concept of a [monolayer](/wiki/Monolayer "Monolayer"), a layer of material one molecule thick. In the early 1950s, Derjaguin and Abrikosova conducted the first measurement of surface forces.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In 1974 the process of [atomic layer deposition](/wiki/Atomic_layer_deposition "Atomic layer deposition") for depositing uniform thin films one atomic layer at a time was developed and patented by Tuomo Suntola and co-workers in Finland.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In another development, the synthesis and properties of semiconductor nanocrystals were studied. This led to a fast increasing number of [semiconductor](/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor") nanoparticles of [quantum dots](/wiki/Quantum_dots "Quantum dots").

### Discovery of fullerenes

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Harold_Kroto_2007.jpg/250px-Harold_Kroto_2007.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/aa/Iijima.jpg)

[Fullerenes](/wiki/Fullerene "Fullerene") were discovered in 1985 by [Harry Kroto](/wiki/Harry_Kroto "Harry Kroto"), [Richard Smalley](/wiki/Richard_Smalley "Richard Smalley"), and [Robert Curl](/wiki/Robert_Curl "Robert Curl"), who together won the 1996 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry"). Smalley's research in physical chemistry investigated formation of inorganic and semiconductor clusters using pulsed molecular beams and time of flight [mass spectrometry](/wiki/Mass_spectrometry "Mass spectrometry"). As a consequence of this expertise, Curl introduced him to Kroto in order to investigate a question about the constituents of astronomical dust. These are carbon rich grains expelled by old stars such as R Corona Borealis. The result of this collaboration was the discovery of C60 and the fullerenes as the third [allotropic](/wiki/Allotropy "Allotropy") form of carbon. Subsequent discoveries included the [endohedral fullerenes](/wiki/Endohedral_fullerenes "Endohedral fullerenes"), and the larger family of fullerenes the following year.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The discovery of [carbon nanotubes](/wiki/Carbon_nanotubes "Carbon nanotubes") is largely attributed to [Sumio Iijima](/wiki/Sumio_Iijima "Sumio Iijima") of [NEC](/wiki/NEC "NEC") in 1991, although carbon nanotubes have been produced and observed under a variety of conditions prior to 1991.[[30]](#cite_note-carbon-30) Iijima's discovery of multi-walled carbon nanotubes in the insoluble material of arc-burned graphite rods in 1991[[31]](#cite_note-31) and Mintmire, Dunlap, and White's independent prediction that if single-walled carbon nanotubes could be made, then they would exhibit remarkable conducting properties[[32]](#cite_note-32) helped create initial interest in carbon nanotubes.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) Nanotube research accelerated greatly following the independent discoveries[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) by Bethune at IBM[[37]](#cite_note-37) and Iijima at NEC[[38]](#cite_note-38) of single-walled carbon nanotubes and methods to specifically produce them by adding transition-metal catalysts to the carbon in an arc discharge.

In the early 1990s, a team of researchers from the [Max Planck Institute for Nuclear Physics](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Nuclear_Physics "Max Planck Institute for Nuclear Physics") and [University of Arizona](/wiki/University_of_Arizona "University of Arizona") discovered how to synthesize and purify large quantities of fullerenes.[[39]](#cite_note-39) This opened the door to their characterization and functionalization by hundreds of investigators in government and industrial laboratories.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) Shortly after, rubidium-[doped](/wiki/Doping_(semiconductor) "Doping (semiconductor)") C60 was found to be a medium-temperature superconductor.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) [Thomas Ebbesen](/wiki/Thomas_Ebbesen "Thomas Ebbesen") and [Pulickel Ajayen](/wiki/Pulickel_Ajayan "Pulickel Ajayan") demonstrated a method to produce carbon nanotubes at scales allowing their properties to be measured in a laboratory.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Researchers have further developed the field of nanotube-based nanotechnology.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) A 2024 review stated that over 5,000 tons of nanotubes were produced annually, with industrial applications including biosensors, satellite sensors, and marine coatings.[[49]](#cite_note-:0-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) Practical challenges in many applications remain, such as the difficulty of retaining their unique properties in composite materials, ensuring chemical stability, and potential for toxicity.[[49]](#cite_note-:0-49)[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51)[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Government and corporate support

### National Nanotechnology Initiative

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Mihail_Roco.jpg/250px-Mihail_Roco.jpg)

The National Nanotechnology Initiative is a [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") federal nanotechnology [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") program. “The NNI serves as the central point of communication, cooperation, and collaboration for all Federal agencies engaged in nanotechnology research, bringing together the expertise needed to advance this broad and complex field."[[56]](#cite_note-56) Its goals are to advance a world-class nanotechnology research and development (R&D) program, foster the transfer of new technologies into products for commercial and public benefit, develop and sustain educational resources, a skilled workforce, and the supporting infrastructure and tools to advance nanotechnology, and support responsible development of nanotechnology. The initiative was spearheaded by [Mihail Roco](/wiki/Mihail_Roco "Mihail Roco"), who formally proposed the National Nanotechnology Initiative to the [Office of Science and Technology Policy](/wiki/Office_of_Science_and_Technology_Policy "Office of Science and Technology Policy") during the Clinton administration in 1999, and was a key architect in its development. He is currently the Senior Advisor for Nanotechnology at the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation"), as well as the founding chair of the [National Science and Technology Council](/wiki/National_Science_and_Technology_Council "National Science and Technology Council") subcommittee on Nanoscale Science, Engineering and Technology.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") advocated [nanotechnology](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology") development. In a 21 January 2000 speech[[58]](#cite_note-58) at the [California Institute of Technology](/wiki/California_Institute_of_Technology "California Institute of Technology"), Clinton said, "Some of our research goals may take twenty or more years to achieve, but that is precisely why there is an important role for the federal government." Feynman's stature and concept of atomically precise fabrication played a role in securing funding for nanotechnology research, as mentioned in President Clinton's speech:

My budget supports a major new National Nanotechnology Initiative, worth $500 million. Caltech is no stranger to the idea of nanotechnology the ability to manipulate matter at the atomic and molecular level. Over 40 years ago, Caltech's own Richard Feynman asked, "What would happen if we could arrange the atoms one by one the way we want them?"[[59]](#cite_note-59)

President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") further increased funding for nanotechnology. On December 3, 2003, Bush [signed into law](/wiki/Signed_into_law "Signed into law") the 21st Century Nanotechnology Research and Development Act,[[60]](#cite_note-60) which authorizes expenditures for five of the participating agencies totaling [US$](/wiki/US$ "US$")3.63 billion over four years.[[61]](#cite_note-61) The NNI budget supplement for Fiscal Year 2009 provides $1.5 billion to the NNI, reflecting steady growth in the nanotechnology investment.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

## Growing public awareness and controversy

### "Why the future doesn't need us"

"Why the future doesn't need us" is an article written by [Bill Joy](/wiki/Bill_Joy "Bill Joy"), then Chief Scientist at [Sun Microsystems](/wiki/Sun_Microsystems "Sun Microsystems"), in the April 2000 issue of *[Wired](/wiki/Wired_(magazine) "Wired (magazine)")* magazine. In the article, he argues that "Our most powerful 21st-century technologies — [robotics](/wiki/Robotics "Robotics"), [genetic engineering](/wiki/Genetic_engineering "Genetic engineering"), and [nanotech](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology") — are threatening to make humans an [endangered species](/wiki/Endangered_species "Endangered species")." Joy argues that developing technologies provide a much greater danger to humanity than any technology before it has ever presented. In particular, he focuses on [genetics](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics"), [nanotechnology](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology") and [robotics](/wiki/Robotics "Robotics"). He argues that 20th-century technologies of destruction, such as the [nuclear bomb](/wiki/Nuclear_bomb "Nuclear bomb"), were limited to large governments, due to the complexity and cost of such devices, as well as the difficulty in acquiring the required materials. He also voices concern about increasing computer power. His worry is that computers will eventually become more intelligent than we are, leading to such [dystopian](/wiki/Dystopian "Dystopian") scenarios as [robot rebellion](/wiki/Cybernetic_revolt "Cybernetic revolt"). He notably quotes the [Unabomber](/wiki/Unabomber "Unabomber") on this topic. After the publication of the article, Bill Joy suggested assessing technologies to gauge their implicit dangers, as well as having scientists refuse to work on technologies that have the potential to cause harm.

In the [AAAS](/wiki/American_Association_for_the_Advancement_of_Science "American Association for the Advancement of Science") Science and Technology Policy Yearbook 2001 article titled *A Response to Bill Joy and the Doom-and-Gloom Technofuturists*, Bill Joy was criticized for having technological tunnel vision on his prediction, by failing to consider social factors.[[63]](#cite_note-63) In [Ray Kurzweil](/wiki/Ray_Kurzweil "Ray Kurzweil")'s *[The Singularity Is Near](/wiki/The_Singularity_Is_Near "The Singularity Is Near")*, he questioned the regulation of potentially dangerous technology, asking "Should we tell the millions of people afflicted with cancer and other devastating conditions that we are canceling the development of all bioengineered treatments because there is a risk that these same technologies may someday be used for malevolent purposes?".

### *Prey*

*Prey* is a 2002 novel by [Michael Crichton](/wiki/Michael_Crichton "Michael Crichton") which features an artificial swarm of nanorobots which develop intelligence and threaten their human inventors. The novel generated concern within the nanotechnology community that the novel could negatively affect public perception of nanotechnology by creating fear of a similar scenario in real life.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

### Drexler–Smalley debate

Richard Smalley, best known for co-discovering the soccer ball-shaped “buckyball” molecule and a leading advocate of nanotechnology and its many applications, was an outspoken critic of the idea of [molecular assemblers](/wiki/Molecular_assembler "Molecular assembler"), as advocated by Eric Drexler. In 2001 he introduced scientific objections to them[[65]](#cite_note-65) attacking the notion of universal assemblers in a 2001 *[Scientific American](/wiki/Scientific_American "Scientific American")* article, leading to a [rebuttal](/wiki/Counterargument "Counterargument") later that year from Drexler and colleagues,[[66]](#cite_note-smalleyresponse-66) and eventually to an exchange of [open letters](/wiki/Open_letter "Open letter") in 2003.[[67]](#cite_note-counterpoint-67)

Smalley criticized Drexler's work on nanotechnology as naive, arguing that chemistry is extremely complicated, reactions are hard to control, and that a universal assembler is science fiction. Smalley believed that such assemblers were not physically possible and introduced scientific objections to them. His two principal technical objections, which he had termed the “fat fingers problem" and the "sticky fingers problem”, argued against the feasibility of molecular assemblers being able to precisely select and place individual atoms. He also believed that Drexler's speculations about apocalyptic dangers of molecular assemblers threaten the public support for development of nanotechnology.

Smalley first argued that "fat fingers" made MNT impossible. He later argued that nanomachines would have to resemble chemical enzymes more than Drexler's assemblers and could only work in water. He believed these would exclude the possibility of "molecular assemblers" that worked by precision picking and placing of individual atoms. Also, Smalley argued that nearly all of modern chemistry involves reactions that take place in a solvent (usually water), because the small molecules of a solvent contribute many things, such as lowering binding energies for [transition states](/wiki/Transition_state "Transition state"). Since nearly all known chemistry requires a solvent, Smalley felt that Drexler's proposal to use a high vacuum environment was not feasible.

Smalley also believed that Drexler's speculations about apocalyptic dangers of self-replicating machines that have been equated with "molecular assemblers" would threaten the public support for development of nanotechnology. To address the debate between Drexler and Smalley regarding molecular assemblers, *[Chemical & Engineering News](/wiki/Chemical_%26_Engineering_News "Chemical & Engineering News")* published a point-counterpoint consisting of an exchange of letters that addressed the issues.[[67]](#cite_note-counterpoint-67)

Drexler and coworkers responded to these two issues[[66]](#cite_note-smalleyresponse-66) in a 2001 publication. Drexler and colleagues noted that Drexler never proposed universal assemblers able to make absolutely anything, but instead proposed more limited assemblers able to make a very wide variety of things. They challenged the relevance of Smalley's arguments to the more specific proposals advanced in *Nanosystems*. Drexler maintained that both were [straw man](/wiki/Straw_man "Straw man") arguments, and in the case of enzymes, Prof. Klibanov wrote in 1994

SUMMARY:
**Erwin Müller**, working at Siemens Research Laboratory, invented the **field emission microscope**, allowing near-atomic-resolution images of materials. The National Nanotechnology Infrastructure Network (NNIN) was established by the U.S. National Science Foundation as a network of user facilities to support research and education in nanoscale science, engineering, and technology. Image 29: IBM Nano World A rendered image of a nanoscale silicon tip chiseling out the smallest relief map of the world from a substrate of organic molecular glass (courtesy of Advanced Materials) Image 30: 2015Credit: Institute for Electronics and Nanotechnology/Georgia Institute of Technology Using a pair of sensors made from carbon nanotubes, researchers from MIT, the Singapore-MIT Alliance for Research and Technology, and the National University of Singapore discovered signals that reveal when plants are experiencing stresses, such as heat, light, or attack from insects or bacteria.

Nanotechnology Timeline
=======================

#### Early Examples of Nanostructured Materials

_Long before modern science, early craftsmen discovered ways to make nanostructured materials with unique properties. Use of high heat was one common step in the processes they developed to produce these materials._

*   **4th Century:** The [**Lycurgus Cup**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lycurgus_Cup "Click to see more!") (Rome) is an example of **dichroic glass**; colloidal gold and silver in the glass allow it to look opaque green when lit from outside but translucent red when light shines through the inside.

![Image 4: Photo of the Lycurgus Cup at the British Museum, lit from without](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/lycurguscup1a.jpg)![Image 5: Photo of the Lycurgus Cup at the British Museum, lit from within](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/lycurguscup2a.jpg)
The Lycurgus Cup at the British Museum, lit from the outside (_**left**_) and from the inside (_**right**_)The Lycurgus Cup at the British Museum, lit from the outside (_**top**_) and from the inside (_**bottom**_)

* * *

*   **9th–17th Centuries:** Glowing, glittering [**“luster” ceramic glazes used in the Islamic world**](http://archaeology.about.com/od/lterms/qt/lustreware.htm "Click to see more!"), and later in Europe, contained silver or copper or other metallic nanoparticles.

![Image 6: Photo, 9th C Iraq lustreware bowl](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/iraq_polychromelustrewarebowl-archaeology.com-s.jpg)
Polychrome lustreware bowl, 9th C, Iraq, British Museum (©_T_ rinitat Pradell 2008)

* * *

*   **6th–15th Centuries:** Vibrant stained glass windows in European cathedrals owed their rich colors to nanoparticles of gold chloride and other metal oxides and chlorides; gold nanoparticles also acted as photocatalytic air purifiers.

![Image 7: Photo, Rose window, Notre Dame Cathedral](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/rosewindownotredame-s.jpg)
The South rose window of Notre Dame Cathedral, CA 1250

* * *

*   **13th–18th Centuries:**[**“Damascus” saber blades**](http://www.nytimes.com/2006/11/28/science/28observ.html?scp=1&sq=Antique+Nanotubes&st=nyt "Click to see more!") contained carbon nanotubes and cementite nanowires—an ultrahigh-carbon steel formulation that gave them strength, resilience, the ability to hold a keen edge, and a visible moiré pattern in the steel that give the blades their name.

![Image 8: Photo, Damascus saber, 17th C](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/damascussaber-s.jpg)![Image 9: Photo, carbon nanotubes in a Damascus sword, 17th C](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/damascusswordcnts-nature444-286-s.jpg)

(**Left**) A Damascus saber (photo by Tina Fineberg for The New York Times). (**Right**) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy image of carbon nanotubes in a genuine Damascus sabre after dissolution in hydrochloric acid, showing remnants of cementite nanowires encapsulated by carbon nanotubes (scale bar, 5 nm) (M. Reibold, P. Paufler, A. A. Levin, W. Kochmann, N. Pätzke & D. C. Meyer, _Nature_ 444, 286, 2006).

(**Top**) A Damascus saber (photo by Tina Fineberg for The New York Times). (**Bottom**) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy image of carbon nanotubes in a genuine Damascus sabre after dissolution in hydrochloric acid, showing remnants of cementite nanowires encapsulated by carbon nanotubes (scale bar, 5 nm) (M. Reibold, P. Paufler, A. A. Levin, W. Kochmann, N. Pätzke & D. C. Meyer, _Nature_ 444, 286, 2006).

#### Examples of Discoveries and Developments Enabling Nanotechnology

_Nanotechnology has significantly evolved over the years. Here are a handful of examples of discoveries and advances that have enabled nanotechnology over time._

1857
----

**Michael Faraday** discovered **colloidal “ruby” gold**, demonstrating that nanostructured gold under certain lighting conditions produces different-colored solutions.

![Image 10: Colloidal Ruby Gold](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/faraday-rubygoldcolloid-s.jpg) Gold Bulletin 2007 40.4, p. 267

1936
----

**Erwin Müller**, working at Siemens Research Laboratory, invented the **field emission microscope**, allowing near-atomic-resolution images of materials.

1947
----

**John Bardeen**, **William Shockley**, and **Walter Brattain** at Bell Labs discovered the **semiconductor transistor** and greatly expanded scientific knowledge of semiconductor interfaces, laying the foundation for electronic devices and the Information Age.

![Image 11: 1947 Transistor Bell Labs](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/1947_transistor-belllabs-alcatel-lucent.jpg) 1947 transistor, Bell Labs

1950
----

**Victor La Mer** and **Robert Dinegar** developed the **theory and a process for growing monodisperse colloidal materials**. Controlled ability to fabricate colloids enables myriad industrial uses such as specialized papers, paints, and thin films, even dialysis treatments.

1951
----

**Erwin Müller** pioneered the **field ion microscope**, a means to image the arrangement of atoms at the surface of a sharp metal tip; he first imaged tungsten atoms.

1956
----

**Arthur von Hippel** at MIT introduced many concepts of—and coined the term—**“molecular engineering”** as applied to dielectrics, ferroelectrics, and piezoelectrics.

1958
----

**Jack Kilby** of Texas Instruments originated the concept of, designed, and built the first **integrated circuit**, for which he received the Nobel Prize in 2000.

![Image 12: Jack Kilby, about 1960](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/kilby-ic-1960-s.jpg) Jack Kilby, about 1960

1959
----

**Richard Feynman** of the California Institute of Technology gave what is considered to be the first lecture on technology and engineering at the atomic scale, [**"There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom"**](http://www.zyvex.com/nanotech/feynman.html "Click to see more!") at an American Physical Society meeting at Caltech.

![Image 13: Richard Feynman](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/feynman-s.jpg) Richard Feynman (Caltech archives)

1965
----

**Gordon Moore**, Intel co-founder, described in _Electronics_ magazine several trends he foresaw in electronics. Known as **Moore’s Law**, he observed the density of transistors on an integrated chip doubling every 12 months (later 24 months) and predicted ongoing shrinkage in chip sizes with increasing performance.

![Image 14: Moore's Law graph](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/mooreslawgraph-intel.jpg) Moore’s first public graph showing his vision of "[cramming more components onto integrated circuits](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/mooreslaw.pdf "Click to see more")"

1974
----

**Norio Taniguchi** of Tokyo Science University coined the **term "nanotechnology"** to describe precision machining of materials to within atomic-scale dimensional tolerances.

1981
----

**Gerd Binnig** and **Heinrich Rohrer** at IBM's Zurich lab invented the **scanning tunneling microscope**, allowing scientists to "see" (create direct spatial images of) individual atoms for the first time. Binnig and Rohrer won the Nobel Prize for this discovery in 1986.

1981
----

Russia's **Alexei Ekimov** discovered nanocrystalline, semiconducting **quantum dots in a glass matrix** and conducted pioneering studies of their electronic and optical properties.

1983
----

Louis Brus (Columbia University) discovered that the properties of colloidal nanoparticles, specifically those made of cadmium sulfide, change as the size of the nanoparticles change.

1985
----

Rice University researchers **Harold Kroto**, **Sean O’Brien**, **Robert Curl**, and **Richard Smalley** discovered the **Buckminsterfullerene** (C60), a molecule resembling a soccer ball composed entirely of carbon. Their work earned the 1996 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.

![Image 15: Buckyball molecule](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/c60buckyball.png) Diagram of the buckyball (C60), a spherical carbon molecule

1985
----

Bell Labs’s **Louis Brus** discovered **colloidal semiconductor nanocrystals (quantum dots)**, for which he later shared the 2008 Kavli Prize in Nanotechnology.

1986
----

**Gerd Binnig**, **Calvin Quate**, and **Christoph Gerber** invented the **atomic force microscope**, which has the capability to view, measure, and manipulate materials down to fractions of a nanometer in size.

1989
----

**Don Eigler** and **Erhard Schweizer** at IBM's Almaden Research Center **manipulated 35 individual xenon atoms to spell out the IBM logo**. This demonstration of the ability to precisely manipulate atoms ushered in the applied use of nanotechnology.

![Image 16: IBM logo atoms](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/ibminxenon-ibmpresskit-s.jpg) IBM logo spelled using 35 xenon atoms manipulated by a scanning tunneling microscope

1991
----

**Sumio Iijima** of NEC discovered the **carbon nanotube (CNT)**. CNTs, like buckyballs, are entirely composed of carbon but have a tubular shape. They exhibit extraordinary properties in strength, electrical, and thermal conductivity, among others.

![Image 17: CNT diagram](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/cnt-smalley-rice-nsf-s.jpg)![Image 18: CNT micrograph](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/cntpaper-grc.nasa_.gov_.jpg)![Image 19: CNT array](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/cntantenna-bostoncollege-s_0.jpg) Various images of carbon nanotubes: diagram, SEM micrograph, and aligned array (courtesy: NSF, NASA, Boston College)

1992
----

**C.T. Kresge** and colleagues at Mobil Oil discovered the **nanostructured catalytic materials MCM-41 and MCM-48**, now used heavily in refining crude oil as well as for drug delivery, water delivery, water treatment, and other varied applications.

![Image 20: MCM-41 TEM Image 1](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/mcm-41-lm-msu-s.jpg)![Image 21: MCM-41 TEM Image 2](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/mcm-41-orthoganal-msu-pauly-s.jpg) MCM 41 is a "mesoporous molecular sieve" silica nanomaterial with a hexagonal or "honeycomb" arrangement of its straight cylindrical pores (courtesy of Thomas Pauly, Michigan State University)

1993
----

Moungi Bawendi (MIT) developed synthesis methods to produce quantum dots of specific sizes, which was necessary for their commercial applications.

1993
----

The National Nanofabrication Users Network(NNUN) was established by the U.S. National Science Foundation to provide users from academia, government, and industry with access to sophisticated nanofabrication technologies.

1993
----

**Moungi Bawendi** of MIT invented a **method for controlled synthesis of nanocrystals** (quantum dots), paving the way for applications ranging from computing to biology to high-efficiency photovoltaics and lighting. Other researchers such as Louis Brus and Chris Murray also contributed methods for synthesizing quantum dots.

1997
----

Researchers at Cornell University made the world's smallest guitar out of crystalline silicon. No larger than a single cell, this "nanoguitar" was 10 micrometers long, with six strings each about 50 nanometers, or 100 atoms, wide.

1998
----

The **Interagency Working Group on Nanotechnology (IWGN)** was formed under the National Science and Technology Council to investigate the state of the art in nanoscale science and technology and to forecast possible future developments. Their report, [**Nanotechnology Research Directions: Vision for the Next Decade**](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/IWGN_rd.pdf "Click to see more!") (1999), defined the vision for and led directly to the formation of the U.S. National Nanotechnology Initiative in 2000.

1999
----

**Cornell University researchers Wilson Ho** and **Hyojune Lee** probed secrets of chemical bonding by **assembling a molecule (iron carbonyl Fe(CO)2)** from constituent components iron (Fe) and carbon monoxide (CO) with a scanning tunneling microscope.

![Image 22: Molecule assembly STM](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/molecularassembly-ct-2.jpg) The progression of steps using a scanning tunneling microscope tip to "assemble" an iron carbonyl molecule (courtesy, Wilson Ho, Science 285, 1719 (1999))

1999
----

**Chad Mirkin** at Northwestern University invented **dip-pen nanolithography** (DPN®), leading to manufacturable, reproducible "writing" of electronic circuits as well as patterning of biomaterials for cell biology research, nanoencryption, and other applications.

![Image 23: Dip-pen nanolithography](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/dpn_multi_matchstick_macro-nanoink2010.jpg) Use of DPN to deposit biomaterials (©2010 Nanoink)

1999–early 2000s
----------------

**Consumer products** making use of nanotechnology began appearing in the marketplace, including lightweight nanotechnology-enabled automobile bumpers that resist denting and scratching, golf balls that fly straighter, tennis rackets that are stiffer, baseball bats with better flex and "kick," nano-silver antibacterial socks, clear sunscreens, wrinkle- and stain-resistant clothing, deep-penetrating therapeutic cosmetics, scratch-resistant glass coatings, faster-recharging batteries for cordless electric tools, and improved displays for televisions, cell phones, and digital cameras.

2000
----

**President Clinton** launched the National Nanotechnology Initiative (NNI) to coordinate Federal R&D efforts and promote U.S. competitiveness in nanotechnology. Congress funded the NNI for the first time in FY2001. The NSET Subcommittee of the NSTC was designated as the interagency group responsible for coordinating the NNI.

2001
----

Initial demonstration of superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs) by scientists at Moscow State Pedagogical University and the University of Rochester. The scientists showed that single photons could generate resistive hotspots in ultrathin superconducting nanowires made of niobium nitride.

2003
----

**Congress** enacted the **21st Century Nanotechnology Research and Development Act** (P.L. 108-153). The act provided a statutory foundation for the NNI, established programs, assigned agency responsibilities, authorized funding levels, and promoted research to address key issues.

2003
----

**Naomi Halas**, **Jennifer West**, **Rebekah Drezek**, and **Renata Pasqualini** at Rice University developed gold nanoshells, which when “tuned” in size to absorb near-infrared light, serve as a platform for the integrated discovery, diagnosis, and treatment of breast cancer without invasive biopsies, surgery, or systemically destructive radiation or chemotherapy.

![Image 24: Gold nanoshells simulation](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/growthofgoldnanoshell-n.halas-gnn-2003.jpg) Computer simulation of growth of gold nanoshell with silica core and outer layer of gold (courtesy H. Halas, Gianna News Network, 2003)

2004
----

In collaboration with the U.S. Food and Drug Administration and the National Institute of Standards and Technology, the National Cancer Institute (which is a part of the National Institutes of Health) launched the [Nanotechnology Characterization Laboratory](https://www.cancer.gov/nano/research/ncl/about "Click to see more") to advance cancer nanotechnology and expedite the development of promising nanomedicines.

![Image 25: 2018](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/1997%20Nanoguitar.gif) Photo by D. Carr and H. Craighead, Cornell University

2004
----

The NNI released its first[Strategic Plan](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/pub_resource/nni_strategic_plan_2004.pdf "https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/pub_resource/nni_strategic_plan_2004.pdf"), which stated that “[the] vision of the National Nanotechnology Initiative (NNI) is a future in which the ability to understand and control matter on the nanoscale leads to a revolution in technology and industry. Toward this vision, the NNI will expedite the discovery, development, and deployment of nanotechnology in order to achieve responsible and sustainable economic benefits, to enhance the quality of life, and to promote national security.”

2004
----

Graphene, a single-atom-thick sheet of carbon atoms, was isolated and identified for the first time by Andre Geim and Konstantin Novoselov, both at the University of Manchester, United Kingdom, by repeatedly peeling layers from graphite using Scotch tape. Their work led to the 2010 Nobel Prize in Physics.

![Image 26](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/2004%20Graphene%20Discovery.png) Credit:Gabriel Hildebrand - [Nobelmuseet](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=56583671)

2004
----

The National Nanotechnology Infrastructure Network (NNIN) was established by the U.S. National Science Foundation as a network of user facilities to support research and education in nanoscale science, engineering, and technology. The NNIN was led by Cornell University.

2005
----

**Erik Winfree** and **Paul Rothemund** from the California Institute of Technology developed theories for **DNA-based computation** and **"algorithmic self-assembly"** in which computations are embedded in the process of nanocrystal growth.

2006
----

**James Tour** and colleagues at Rice University built a **nanoscale car** made of oligo(phenylene ethynylene) with alkynyl axles and four spherical C60 fullerene (buckyball) wheels. Increases in temperature caused the nanocar to move about on a gold surface as a result of buckyball wheels turning, as in a conventional car.

![Image 27: Nanocar with buckyball wheels](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/nanocar-rsc-29mar2006.jpg) Nanocar with turning buckyball wheels (credit: RSC, 29 March 2006)

2007
----

The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) established the [NIST Center for Nanoscale Science and Technology](https://www.nist.gov/publications/nist-center-nanoscale-science-and-technology-supporting-us-innovation-nanotechnology "Click to see more") to accelerate innovation in nanotechnology-based commerce.

2007–2008
---------

The five Nanoscale Science Research Centers (NSRCs), user facilities established by the U.S. Department of Energy, began full operation. These centers have been open to the scientific community to perform and collaborate on nanoscience research.

![Image 28: 2007-2008
    ](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/2007-2008%20NSRCs%20CFN.jpg)Credit: Center for Functional Nanomaterials/Brookhaven National Laboratory

2009–2010
---------

**Nadrian Seeman** and colleagues at New York University created several [**DNA-like robotic nanoscale assembly devices**](https://www.nyu.edu/about/news-publications/news/2010/june/nyu-chemist-seeman-wins-kavli-prize-in-nanoscience-.html "Click to see more!"). One is a process for creating 3D DNA structures using synthetic sequences of DNA crystals that can be programmed to self-assemble using “sticky ends” and placement in a set order and orientation. Seeman shared the 2010 Kavli Prize in Nanoscience for this work.

2010
----

**IBM** used a silicon tip measuring only a few nanometers at its apex to chisel away material from a substrate and create a complete nanoscale 3D relief map of the world, one-one thousandth the size of a grain of salt. This activity **demonstrated a powerful patterning methodology** for generating nanoscale patterns and structures as small as 15 nanometers at greatly reduced cost and complexity.

![Image 29: IBM Nano World](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/ibm_3d_map-advancedmaterialsmag.jpg) A rendered image of a nanoscale silicon tip chiseling out the smallest relief map of the world from a substrate of organic molecular glass (courtesy of Advanced Materials)

2011
----

MXenes, a family of two-dimensional layered materials composed of transition metal carbides, nitrides, and carbonitrides, were discovered for the first time by a team of researchers at Drexel University led by Yury Gogotsi and Michel Barsoum. They have a unique combination of properties, including high electrical conductivity, hydrophilicity, large surface area, and tunability, which make them promising for energy storage applications, water purification, gas sensors, and triboelectric nanogenerators. [MXene: A Star is Born](https://exelmagazine.org/article/mxene-a-2d-star-is-born/ "Click to see more!").

2012
----

**George Church** of Harvard University and colleagues published the first scientific article on DNA data storage: [Next-Generation Digital Information Storage in DNA](https://arep.med.harvard.edu/pdf/Church_Science_12.pdf "Click to see more!").

2015
----

The National Nanotechnology Coordinated Infrastructure (NNCI) was established by the U.S. National Science Foundation as the successor to the National Nanotechnology Infrastructure Network (NNIN). NNCI is comprised of 16 user facility sites, their affiliated partners, and a coordinating office at Georgia Tech.

![Image 30: 2015](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/2015%20Cleanroom%20SENIC%20NNCI.jpg)Credit: Institute for Electronics and Nanotechnology/Georgia Institute of Technology

2016
----

Sir J. Fraser Stoddart, Jean-Pierre Sauvage, and Bernard L. Feringa won the [2016 Nobel Prize in Chemistry](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2016/popular-information/ "Click to see more") for their design and production of molecular machines, which are made by linking molecules together.

![Image 31: 2018](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/2016%20Nobel%20Prize%20in%20Chemistry.jpg) Copyright Nobel Media 2016

2016
----

Researchers at Harvard University demonstrated the first planar lens that works with high efficiency within the visible light spectrum. The lens, called a metalens, consists of titanium dioxide nanostructures on a glass substrate. [Metalens works in the visible spectrum, sees smaller than a wavelength of light](https://seas.harvard.edu/news/2016/06/metalens-works-visible-spectrum-sees-smaller-wavelength-light "Click to see more!")

![Image 32: IBM Nano World](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/2016%20Metalens%20Harvard.png)Image courtesy of the Capasso Lab at Harvard University

2016
----

Scientists at the Connecticut Agricultural Experiment Station in New Haven, CT, conducted experiments to stimulate plant immunity to fight fungal infections by supplying nanoscale particles of essential micronutrients, such as copper, zinc, and manganese. (See the following story for additional details:[In Connecticut, a Nanoscale Agricultural Experiment With Global Potential](https://urldefense.com/v3/__https:/undark.org/2017/08/30/nanoscale-connecticut-agricultural-station/__;!!EAPaXxOOW7smCwU!iBGjHnymI4lMzK682KMntwh3LEj1blvv6qEBGLa9bLQaRkgMvrGUGx5_2TfpGrb9RZZDufhNHt3yfTJygtybkg$ "Click to see more!").) In 2024, the scientists launched a company,[Convergent Bio](https://urldefense.com/v3/__https:/convergent-bio.com/about__;!!EAPaXxOOW7smCwU!lMbzu-5w-ShA4bxpuAHzAGWnkTjEv9ITetRXmY0kGhmBl-MH7R2C7K_nA4YkZ30EMwP8ujIOXMuQqjZv8V1OqA$ "Click to see more!"),whose mission is to “create new sustainable, clean agriculture systems which transform our food supply to be better, cleaner and healthier for people and the planet."

2016
----

A research team led by Berkeley Lab created a transistor with a working 1-nanometer gate, breaking the size barrier set by the laws of physics. [Smallest. Transistor. Ever.](https://newscenter.lbl.gov/2016/10/06/smallest-transistor-1-nm-gate/ "Click to see more!")

2018
----

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved Patisiran (ONPATTRO), a lipid nanoparticle small interfering RNA therapeutic for the treatment of amyloidosis, a group of diseases characterized by the abnormal buildup of amyloid proteins in various tissues and organs.

2018
----

The magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene was discovered by a team led by Pablo Jarillo-Herrero at MIT.The team showed that when two graphene sheets are stacked on top of each other at the “magic” angle of 1.1 degrees, the material exhibits unique properties, including superconductivity. [Insulator or superconductor? Physicists find graphene is both.](https://news.mit.edu/2018/graphene-insulator-superconductor-0305 "Click to see more!")

![Image 33: 2018](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/2018%20MIT-Magic-Angle.jpg) Credit: Pablo Jarillo-Herrero and his research team at MIT

2019
----

**Prof. Molly Stevens** from Imperial College of London and **Prof. Sangeeta Bhatia** from MIT developed a novel diagnostic tool that changes color in the presence of cancer, using nanosensors attached to gold nanoclusters. [Colour-change urine test for cancer shows potential in mouse study](https://news.mit.edu/2019/colour-change-urine-cancer-test-0327 "Click to see more!").

2020
----

Scientists at Rice University introduced a new process that can turn bulk quantities of just about any carbon source into graphene flakes. Called the "flash graphene" technique, it can convert coal, food waste, or plastic into graphene for a fraction of the cost used by other bulk graphene-producing methods. [Rice lab turns trash into valuable graphene in a flash](https://news.rice.edu/news/2020/rice-lab-turns-trash-valuable-graphene-flash "Click to see more!")

![Image 34: 2020](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/2020%20Flash%20Graphene.jpg)Photo by Jeff Fitlow; Rice University

2020
----

Lipid nanoparticle-based mRNA vaccines against COVID-19 were produced by Moderna and Pfizer–BioNTech and then approved in the United States under emergency use authorization by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration.

2020
----

MIT researchers demonstrated that carbon nanotube field-effect transistors can be fabricated into circuits more efficiently, accelerating potential commercial adoption. [Carbon nanotube transistors make the leap from lab to factory floor](https://news.mit.edu/2020/carbon-nanotube-transistors-factory-0601 "Click to see more!").

![Image 35: Richard Feynman](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/2020%20Carbon%20Nanotube%20Transistors.jpg)Credit: Max Shulaker and Mindy Bishop / MIT

2021
----

**IBM** announced the world’s first 2-nanometer chip technology, opening new horizons for faster, more efficient semiconductors.

2023
----

Researchers from the National Institute of Standards and Technology and NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory built a superconducting camera containing 400,000 pixels—400 times more than any other device of its type. [NIST Team Develops Highest-Resolution Single-Photon Superconducting Camera](https://www.nist.gov/news-events/news/2023/10/nist-team-develops-highest-resolution-single-photon-superconducting-camera "Click to see more!").

![Image 36: 2023](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/2023%20Superconducting%20Camera.png)Credit: S. Kelley / National Institute of Standards and Technology

2023
----

**Moungi G. Bawendi** (MIT), **Louis E. Brus** (Columbia University), and **Alexei I. Ekimov** (Nanocrystals Technology Inc.) were awarded the 2023 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the discovery and synthesis of nanoparticles called quantum dots. [They planted an important seed for nanotechnology](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2023/summary/ "Click to see more!").

![Image 37: 2023](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/2023%20Chemistry%20Nobel%20Prize%20Winners.png)Chemistry Nobel Prize Winners [Copyright Nobel Prize Outreach]

2023
----

Katalin Kariko and Drew Weissman win Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine "for their discoveries concerning nucleoside base modifications," which led to the development of lipid nanoparticle-based mRNA vaccines against COVID-19.[Katalin Karikó and Drew Weissman, Penn’s Historic mRNA Vaccine Research Team, Win 2023 Nobel Prize in Medicine](https://www.pennmedicine.org/news/news-releases/2023/october/katalin-kariko-and-drew-weissman-win-2023-nobel-prize-in-medicine "Click to see more!")

![Image 38: 2023](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/2023%20Drew%20Weissman%20Katalin%20Kariko.png)Drew Weissman Katalin Kariko [Photo Credit: Peggy Peterson Photography for Penn Medicine]

2023
----

YouTuber Mark Rober posted a 21-minute video,[World's Smallest Nerf Gun Shoots an Ant](https://nano.us21.list-manage.com/track/click?u=71a984bb98dbdd85ae6f52818&id=ce5d8e8a17&e=81a38fda8c "Click to see more!"), in which he and various scientists create Nerf guns that are smaller and smaller in size–including a Nerf gun made with carbon nanotubes and another made with DNA.

![Image 39: 2023](https://www.nano.gov/sites/default/files/2025-05/2023%20YouTuber%20Mark%20Rober.png)YouTuber Mark Rober

2024
----

# Nanotechnology History

Explore diverse perspectives on nanotechnology with structured content covering applications, innovations, challenges, and future trends across industries.

![](https://p16-hera-sg.larksuitecdn.com/tos-alisg-i-hn4qzgxq2n-sg/8055f6941c2640fb974b4475dff17541~tplv-hn4qzgxq2n-png:0:0.png)

Nanotechnology, the science of manipulating matter at the atomic and molecular scale, has revolutionized industries, reshaped scientific research, and opened doors to innovations once thought impossible. While the term "nanotechnology" may evoke images of futuristic devices and cutting-edge materials, its roots stretch back centuries, intertwining with the history of human curiosity and ingenuity. From ancient artisans unknowingly using nanoscale techniques to modern scientists engineering molecular machines, the journey of nanotechnology is a testament to humanity's relentless pursuit of understanding and harnessing the unseen. This article delves into the rich history of nanotechnology, exploring its foundational concepts, pivotal milestones, and transformative impact across industries. Whether you're a seasoned professional or a curious learner, this comprehensive guide will provide actionable insights into the past, present, and future of nanotechnology.

Implement [Nanotechnology] solutions to revolutionize cross-industry innovation and efficiency.

## Understanding the basics of nanotechnology

Understanding the basics of nanotechnology

### Key Concepts and Definitions

Nanotechnology is the science, engineering, and application of materials and devices at the nanoscale, typically between 1 and 100 nanometers. To put this into perspective, a nanometer is one-billionth of a meter—about 100,000 times smaller than the width of a human hair. At this scale, materials exhibit unique physical, chemical, and biological properties that differ significantly from their bulk counterparts. These properties include enhanced strength, increased reactivity, and novel optical behaviors.

Understanding these concepts is crucial for grasping the transformative potential of nanotechnology across various fields.

## Faqs about nanotechnology

Faqs about nanotechnology

### What are the main benefits of nanotechnology?

Nanotechnology offers numerous benefits, including improved medical treatments, enhanced energy efficiency, and the development of stronger, lighter materials. Its ability to manipulate matter at the atomic level enables innovations that were previously unattainable.

### How is nanotechnology impacting daily life?

Nanotechnology impacts daily life in various ways, from the miniaturization of electronic devices to the development of advanced medical therapies and sustainable energy solutions. It also plays a role in consumer products, such as stain-resistant fabrics and scratch-resistant coatings.

### What industries are most influenced by nanotechnology?

Industries most influenced by nanotechnology include healthcare, electronics, energy, and materials science. Its applications range from targeted drug delivery and quantum computing to renewable energy and advanced manufacturing.

### Are there any risks associated with nanotechnology?

Yes, nanotechnology poses risks such as potential toxicity, environmental impact, and ethical concerns. Addressing these risks requires rigorous testing, regulatory oversight, and responsible innovation.

### How can I learn more about nanotechnology?

To learn more about nanotechnology, consider exploring academic courses, attending industry conferences, and reading scientific journals. Online resources, such as webinars and tutorials, also provide valuable insights into this rapidly evolving field.

enriched_points.expanded_content: The concept of nanotechnology can be traced back to ancient artisans who unknowingly utilized nanoscale materials in practices like pottery and metalsmithing, enhancing properties through size manipulation.

Significant breakthroughs include Richard Feynman's 1959 lecture inspiring molecular manipulation, Norio Taniguchi coining 'nanotechnology' in 1974, and the invention of scanning tunneling microscopes in the early 1980s.

From initial theoretical concepts, nanotechnology evolved into practical applications in medicine, electronics, and materials science, including drug delivery systems, nanoscale transistors, and advanced coatings.

Today, nanotechnology is a rapidly advancing field with diverse applications, supported by government initiatives like the National Nanotechnology Initiative, and ongoing research in areas like nanomedicine and sustainable energy.
